subject: Math
sample_standards: Model with mathematics.
Attend to precision.
Numbers and Operations
Engage in 1:1 correspondence.
Interact with a drawing.
Write the numbers 0-100.
Engage with numbers
Write the numbers 0-10.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Numbers and Operations — Number Representation and Precision
coherence_reason: The standards relate to numbers and mathematical practices, but mix general process standards (modeling, precision) with specific number representation skills.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can listen carefully and talk with others to learn new things.
Your child enjoys practicing counting from 1 to 20 using songs, rhymes, or tools.
Your child can count out loud, sign, or use assistive tools quickly and accurately.
Your child takes part in math practice activities, using assistive devices if needed.
Your child can practice counting and math skills using songs, rhymes, and tools.
Your child can count out loud, sign, or use assistive technology to count quickly and accurately.
Your child takes part in math practice activities, using special devices if needed.
Your child enjoys practicing math skills through songs, rhymes, or using tools.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Numbers and Counting — Counting Strategies and Participation
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on counting and participation using various strategies and tools, but some mention general engagement or learning from discussion, introducing minor variation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find the hour on a digital clock that matches a regular activity.
Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes on both analog and digital clocks, using a.m. and p.m.
Your child can read and write the time to the nearest minute on both analog and digital clocks and knows the difference between a.m. and p.m.
Your child knows who the leaders are in your town and state and what they do.
Your child can read and write time to the exact minute on both analog and digital clocks and knows the difference between a.m. and p.m.
Your child can tell and write the time to the nearest five minutes using both a.m. and p.m. on analog and digital clocks.
Your child can tell time to the hour and half-hour on both analog and digital clocks and learn about a.m. and p.m.
Your child can tell and write time to the nearest five minutes on clocks and use terms like a.m., p.m., half past, and quarter til.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Time — Reading Clocks and Using A.M./P.M.
coherence_reason: All standards focus on reading and writing time to the nearest five minutes or minute using analog and digital clocks, and understanding a.m. and p.m.; the one non-math standard about community leaders is clearly an outlier.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can show how to line up an object starting at zero on a measuring tool.
Your child can find zero on a measuring tool to start measuring an object.
Your child knows to start measuring at the 0 mark on a measuring tool.
Your child knows that measuring starts at 0 on the measuring tool.
Your child can line up objects at 0 on a measuring tool before measuring.
Your child knows that 0 on a measuring tool is where you start measuring.
Your child will show how to line up an object starting at zero on a measuring tool.
Your child understands that measuring length starts at 0 on the tool.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Measurement — Starting at Zero on Measuring Tools
coherence_reason: All standards are about the concept of starting measurement at zero on a measuring tool, with no ambiguity or additional topics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can guess the length of objects using units like inches, feet, centimeters, or meters.
Your child can measure the length of several objects or the same object many times, then show these measurements on a line plot using whole numbers.
Your child can make something longer or shorter by adding or taking away units.
Your child can measure how long something is by using objects like blocks or paper clips.
Your child can put objects in order by length using everyday items to measure.
Your child can measure and estimate lengths using standard units.
Your child can measure how much longer one object is than another and say the difference using standard units like inches or centimeters.
Your child can choose and use tools like rulers or measuring tapes to measure how long things are.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Measurement — Measuring Lengths with Standard Units
coherence_reason: All standards focus on measuring, comparing, estimating, and representing length using standard units and appropriate tools.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can make picture graphs using measurement data they have collected.
Represent and interpret data.
Your child can make picture and bar graphs with up to four groups and use them to solve simple problems about adding, taking away, or comparing amounts.
Your child can look at a graph and use the information to make conclusions and guesses about what might happen next.
Your child can organize information into up to four groups using picture graphs and bar graphs with intervals of one or more.
Your child understands that the length of a bar in a bar graph or the number of pictures in a picture graph shows how many items are in each group.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
Your child can create and use pictures or notes to organize and explain math ideas.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Data — Picture and Bar Graphs
coherence_reason: Most standards are about collecting, representing, and interpreting data with picture and bar graphs, but two standards mention timelines, calendars, and general representation methods, which are broader.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.
Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.
Your child can give presentations that stay on topic and be ready to talk about them.
Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.
Your child can choose and use the right tools to help solve math problems.
Your child can choose the right tools like objects, paper, or technology, and use methods like mental math or estimation to solve problems.
Your child can explain and support their math ideas clearly using correct math words, both in writing and speaking.
Your child can solve problems by understanding the information, making a plan, finding an answer, explaining it, and checking if it makes sense.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Mathematical Practices — Problem Solving and Communication
coherence_reason: All standards focus on mathematical practices such as problem solving, use of tools, and clear communication of mathematical thinking.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can split an even number of objects into two equal groups.
Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number by pairing them or counting by twos, and write an equation showing an even number as two equal groups added together.
Your child can tell if a number up to 40 is even or odd by pairing objects to represent the number.
Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects is odd or even by pairing or counting by twos, and write an equation showing two equal groups for even numbers.
Your child can understand odd and even numbers by pairing objects into two equal groups or two groups with one left over, using up to 20 objects.
Your child can show even numbers as two equal groups and odd numbers as two equal groups plus one extra.
Your child can tell if a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number.
Your child can write an addition equation showing an even number as two equal parts added together.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Numbers — Odd and Even Numbers
coherence_reason: All standards address identifying, representing, and reasoning about odd and even numbers by grouping, pairing, and writing equations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.
Your child uses specific skills to understand and read stories and poems smoothly.
Your child can use specific skills to understand and read informational texts smoothly.
Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.
Your child can notice and explain patterns or repeated steps when solving problems.
Your child understands and uses the chain rule to find derivatives of combined functions.
Your child can notice and use the way math problems are put together to help solve them.
Your child understands why people choose to live in certain places because of the environment.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Problem Solving — Using Structure and Patterns
coherence_reason: The cluster includes standards about math problem structure and patterns, but also contains standards about reading fluency, calculus, and social studies, which are unrelated.
split_topics: Math: Using structure and patterns in problem solving

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how Alabama’s five geographic regions affected where settlers moved in the early 1800s.
Your child understands that the more fractional parts used to make a whole, the smaller each part is, and the fewer parts, the bigger each part is.
Your child understands what rights people have and what they should do as citizens in your community and state.
Your child understands the formal way to define a derivative at a point and what it means for a function to be differentiable.
Your child understands that plant and animal cells have many genes, usually two copies of each, which can affect traits differently.
Your child understands that equal parts of the same whole can look different in shape.
Your child understands that a quarter means one of four equal parts.
Fraction: Part of a whole.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Fractions — Equal Parts and Fraction Concepts
coherence_reason: Three standards are about fractions and equal parts, but other unrelated standards about geography, citizenship, calculus, and genetics are included.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can divide circles and rectangles into 2, 3, or 4 equal parts, name these parts as halves, thirds, or fourths, and understand that equal parts can look different but still be the same size.
Your child can divide a rectangle into equal rows and columns of squares and count how many squares there are in total.
Your child can use objects to count fractional parts beyond one whole and say how many parts make a whole.
Your child can cut or divide flat shapes like rectangles and squares into parts and name the smaller shapes created.
Your child learns about shapes by looking at their parts and how they fit together, and practices dividing shapes to understand fractions.
Your child can show, create, and explain division by splitting a group of objects into equal groups.
Your child can recognize examples and non-examples of halves, fourths, and eighths.
Your child can find the area of a rectangle by covering it with square units, counting them without gaps or overlaps, and telling how many squares cover it.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Geometry — Partitioning Shapes and Understanding Fractions
coherence_reason: The cluster includes both partitioning shapes to understand fractions and concepts related to area and arrays, which are related but distinct mathematical ideas.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can name common flat shapes like squares, circles, triangles, and rectangles.

Your child can recognize and draw shapes with certain features, like a specific number of angles or equal sides, and can identify shapes like triangles, quadrilaterals, pentagons, hexagons, and cubes.
Your child understands how to think about shapes and their features.
Your child can sort and arrange polygons with up to 12 sides by their features, including counting their sides and corners.
Your child uses tools to identify, describe, and draw special four-sided shapes and sorts flat shapes based on their features.
Your child can make flat shapes based on given features, like how many sides and corners they have.
Your child uses tools to explore the features of triangles and circles.
Your child can sort and group 3D shapes like spheres, cones, cylinders, rectangular prisms (including cubes), and triangular prisms by their features using correct shape words.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Geometry — Shapes and Their Attributes
coherence_reason: All standards focus on recognizing, drawing, describing, and sorting both 2D and 3D shapes by their attributes such as sides, angles, and features.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve money word problems using dollar bills and coins, and write amounts with $ and ¢ symbols correctly.
Work with time and money.
Your child understands that money is worth something.
Your child can tell the difference between putting money in a bank (deposit) and taking money out (withdrawal).
Your child can tell the difference between producers and consumers and figure out how much it costs to make a simple item.
Your child can use symbols for cents, dollars, and decimal points to say how much a group of coins is worth.
Your child can recognize examples of borrowing money and think about the benefits and costs when deciding to borrow.
Your child understands that instead of spending money, they can save it.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Money — Value, Transactions, and Financial Decision-Making
coherence_reason: Standards cover both mathematical skills with money (counting, symbols, solving problems) and broader financial literacy concepts (saving, borrowing, producers/consumers), indicating two distinct sub-concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use real objects to put together and break apart numbers up to 10 in different ways.
Your child can show numbers up to 30 by making groups of tens and ones with objects.
Your child can compare groups or numbers using words like more, less, or equal.
Your child works with equal groups of objects to start learning multiplication.
Your child can show and explain multiplication by grouping equal sets of objects in real-life situations.
Your child works with equal groups of objects to start learning about multiplication.
Your child can work with equal groups of objects to learn the basics of multiplication.
Your child can work with equal groups of objects to get ready for learning multiplication.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Operations and Algebraic Thinking — Introduction to Multiplication with Equal Groups
coherence_reason: The cluster includes standards about composing/decomposing numbers, grouping tens/ones, comparing numbers, as well as several about introducing multiplication with equal groups, which are related but distinct early math concepts.
split_topics: Composing and decomposing numbers, grouping tens and ones, comparing numbers
Introduction to multiplication with equal groups

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rows and columns (up to 5 rows and 5 columns) and write an equation showing the total as equal groups added together.
Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects in equal groups, up to 10 items.
Collaboration
Your child can use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rows and columns (up to 5 rows and 5 columns) and write an equation showing equal groups added together.
Your child can use repeated addition to find the total number of objects in equal groups or rows, with totals up to 20.
Your child can use repeated addition to find the total number of items in equal groups and show this with arrays and equations.
Your child can add up objects arranged in rows and columns (up to 5 by 5) and write an addition equation showing equal groups.
Your child can show a multiplication problem using groups of objects, like 4 groups of 2 pencils.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Foundations of Multiplication — Repeated Addition and Arrays
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on using repeated addition and arrays to represent equal groups, though one mentions showing multiplication problems and one is unrelated (Collaboration).

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can show whole numbers on a number line and use the line to add and subtract numbers up to 100.
Your child can use a number line to add one more unit of length.
Your child can show how whole numbers can represent distances on a number line in any position.
Your child can find where a given whole number is located on an open number line.
Your child can name the whole number that matches a specific point on a number line.
Your child can show whole numbers on a number line starting at zero and use the number line to add and subtract numbers up to 100.
Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.
Your child can put numbers up to 100 in order, compare them, and place them on a number line.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Number Lines — Representing, Locating, and Operating with Whole Numbers
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on using number lines for whole numbers, operations, and ordering, but one introduces coordinate pairs and intersecting number lines (a distinct concept).
split_topics: Using number lines for whole numbers and operations
Introduction to coordinate pairs on intersecting number lines

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows what the plus (+), minus (–), and equals (=) signs mean.
Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at the hundreds, tens, and ones digits and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
Your child can compare and order whole numbers up to 1,200 using place value and symbols like >, <, or =.
Your child can place, order, and compare whole numbers up to 1,000.
Your child can compare two three-digit numbers by looking at their hundreds, tens, and ones digits and use >, =, or < to show which is bigger or if they are equal.
Your child can compare one-digit numbers using >, =, and < symbols (like 6 > 2).
Your child can compare two-digit numbers by looking at each digit and using >, =, and < symbols (like 56 > 52).
Equal to: The same value as.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Place Value — Comparing and Ordering Numbers
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on comparing and ordering numbers using place value and comparison symbols, but a couple address knowing what the symbols mean in general.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows that 100 is the same as a bundle of ten tens, called a 'hundred.'
Your child understands that a three-digit number shows how many hundreds, tens, and ones it has, like 706 means 7 hundreds, 0 tens, and 6 ones.
Understand place value.
Your child understands that numbers like 100, 200, 300, and so on mean one, two, three, etc., hundreds with zero tens and ones.
Your child understands how the War of 1812 and the Creek War affected Alabama socially, politically, and economically.
Your child understands why Europeans explored and settled in Alabama and how this affected trade, health, and land.
Your child knows that DNA is the genetic material found in the chromosomes inside every cell.
Your child can show and understand the place value of hundreds, tens, and ones in three-digit numbers.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Place Value — Hundreds, Tens, and Ones
coherence_reason: Most standards are about place value in math, but a few are unrelated social studies and science concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can read and write numbers up to 1000 in regular form, word form, and expanded form.
Your child can write numbers up to 1,200 in standard and other common forms.
Your child can read, write, and put in order numbers up to 1000 using digits, words, and expanded form.
Your child can read and write numbers from 0 to 100 in normal form and in expanded form (showing tens and ones).
Your child can read and write numbers from 0 to 1,000 in regular numbers, broken down parts, and words.
Your child can use place value to read, write, compare, and understand numbers up to 1,000.
Your child can read and write numbers up to 1,000 using digits, words, and expanded form.
Your child can read, write, and show three-digit numbers in normal form, broken down form, and word form using pictures or objects.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Place Value — Reading and Writing Numbers to 1,000
coherence_reason: All standards focus on reading and writing numbers up to 1,000 in different forms (standard, word, expanded), with minor variations in upper limit and method, but the concept is unified.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can say what number comes next between 1 and 10.
Your child can recognize and name numbers from 1 to 30.
Your child can count from 1 to 30 and knows what the last number means.
Your child can count numbers up to 1000 and skip-count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
Your child can make a number that is either bigger or smaller than a given whole number up to 1,200.
Your child can count to 1000 and skip count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
Your child can count up to 1000 and skip count by 5s, 10s, and 100s.
Your child can read, write, and skip count numbers up to 1000 using place value.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Numbers and Counting — Reading, Writing, and Skip Counting up to 1000
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on reading, writing, and skip counting numbers up to 1000, but a few reference lower number ranges (1-10, 1-30) or generating greater/lesser numbers, which are minor variations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers using place value and math rules.
Your child can add up to four two-digit numbers and subtract two-digit numbers using mental strategies and written methods based on place value.
Your child can use a method to add or subtract a two-digit number between 11 and 19 and a one-digit number.
Your child can explain or show that + means putting numbers together using models.
Your child adds two two-digit numbers using strategies like drawings or breaking numbers apart.
Your child adds three two-digit numbers using strategies like drawings or breaking numbers apart.
Your child can solve addition and subtraction problems with two-digit numbers by breaking numbers into parts.
Your child can add up to three two-digit numbers using place value and math properties.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Two-Digit Numbers and Place Value Strategies
coherence_reason: All standards focus on addition and subtraction of two-digit numbers using place value and various strategies, but there is minor variation in the number of addends and inclusion of some one-digit numbers.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how addition and subtraction relate to measuring length.
Your child can solve word problems about lengths using addition and subtraction within 100, with drawings or equations that include an unknown number.
Your child can solve word problems about length using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 100, and write equations with a symbol for the unknown number.
Your child can solve simple real-life problems by adding or subtracting lengths up to 20 using the same units like inches, feet, or yards.
Your child can use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve word problems about lengths measured in the same units.
Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve problems involving length.
Your child can solve addition word problems about length.
Your child can solve addition and subtraction word problems about lengths.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Measurement — Addition and Subtraction of Lengths
coherence_reason: All standards focus on solving addition and subtraction problems involving measurement of length, with only minor differences in wording or context.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can show and solve problems that use addition and subtraction.
Your child can solve one- and two-step word problems using addition and subtraction within 100, with unknown numbers anywhere in the problem, using drawings or equations.
Your child can solve one-step and multi-step word problems with addition and subtraction up to 1,000 using different methods.
Your child can solve addition and subtraction word problems where any part of the problem might be missing.
Your child can use addition and subtraction to solve different kinds of problems.
Your child can use addition and subtraction to estimate and solve one- and two-step word problems with numbers up to 100, using objects, drawings, and equations with an unknown number.
Your child can solve one- and two-step real-life problems using addition and subtraction.
Your child can find the missing number in addition or subtraction equations where the unknown can be in any spot.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Word Problems and Unknowns
coherence_reason: All standards describe solving one- and two-step word problems using addition and subtraction, including finding unknowns in equations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child uses place value and math rules to add and subtract numbers.
Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 1000 using drawings or objects and understand how to add or subtract hundreds, tens, and ones.
Your child can explain why their ways of adding and subtracting work by using place value and math rules.
Your child can use objects, pictures, and numbers from 0 to 20 to add and subtract.
Your child can use objects and pictures to build and break apart numbers up to 1,200 in different ways using thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones.
Your child can create and solve math problems using addition and subtraction with numbers up to 1,000.
Your child can solve one-step and multi-step addition and subtraction problems up to 1,000 using different methods based on place value.
Your child can use place value to find out if a number is 10 or 100 more or less than another number up to 1,200.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Place Value — Addition and Subtraction within 1,000
coherence_reason: Nearly all standards address addition and subtraction within 1,000 using place value, but a few also include building/breaking numbers and comparing numbers with 10/100 more or less, which are adjacent but slightly different skills.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 100 using place value and math rules.
Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 100 using place value, math rules, and the connection between addition and subtraction.
Your child can add and subtract whole numbers up to 100 accurately.
Your child can practice adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number with answers up to 100, and subtracting a one-digit number from a two-digit number.
Your child can solve addition and subtraction problems with numbers up to 100.
Your child can add and subtract numbers up to 50 using place value strategies like grouping tens and ones.
Your child connects the minus sign (-) with words like take away and subtract.
Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 100 using place value and other strategies.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Within 100 Using Place Value Strategies
coherence_reason: All standards address addition and subtraction within 100, primarily emphasizing place value and mental strategies; one standard focuses on connecting notation to vocabulary, which is a slight variation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers within 20 in their head and knows all sums of two one-digit numbers by memory.
Add and subtract within 20.
Your child remembers basic addition and subtraction facts up to 20 without needing to think hard.
Your child can quickly add and subtract numbers up to 20 in their head and knows all sums of two one-digit numbers from memory.
Your child can find the missing number in simple addition or subtraction problems with sums up to 20.
Your child can remember addition facts that add up to 10 and the related subtraction facts.
Your child can decide if simple addition or subtraction equations with up to three numbers are true or false, with sums up to 20.
Your child can solve simple real-life addition and subtraction problems within 20 using objects.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Addition and Subtraction — Fluency within 20
coherence_reason: All standards are focused on students' fluency in addition and subtraction within 20, including mental math, memorization, solving real-life problems, and equations.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can quickly add or subtract 10 or 100 to numbers between 100 and 900 in their head.
Your child can round numbers from 0 to 100 to the nearest ten.
Your child can find the number that is ten more or ten less than a given two-digit number.
Your child can round numbers from 0 to 100 to the nearest 10 using pictures or visual help.
Your child can find numbers that are 10 more, 10 less, 100 more, or 100 less than a given three-digit number.
Your child can add or subtract 10 or 100 in their head from numbers between 100 and 900.
Your child can add or subtract 10 from numbers up to 100.
Add tens on their own.
canonical_name: Grade 2 Math: Place Value — Rounding and Adding/Subtracting Tens and Hundreds
coherence_reason: The cluster contains both rounding to the nearest ten and mental addition/subtraction of tens and hundreds, which are related but distinct sub-concepts within place value.
